1.description: Excess returns of sorts on short-term leverage and long-term leverage. We plot the excess returns from independent2×3×3$2\times 3\times 3$sorts on size (ME$ME$), short-term leverage (STLEV$STLEV$), and long-term leverage (LTLEV$LTLEV$).STLEV$STLEV$is leverage based on debt maturing in the next three years, andLTLEV$LTLEV$is leverage based on debt maturing after three years. Each month, we use the median NYSE market capitalization to split firms into two groups, small and big; independently, to sort stocks into threeSTLEV$STLEV$groups using the NYSE breakpoints for the low 30%, middle 40%, and high 30% of the rankedSTLEV$STLEV$; and independently, to sort stocks into threeLTLEV$LTLEV$groups using the NYSE breakpoints for the low 30%, middle 40%, and high 30% of the rankedLTLEV$LTLEV$. Taking the intersections of the two size, threeSTLEV$STLEV$, and threeLTLEV$LTLEV$groups, we compute the monthly value-weighted returns of the2×3×3=18$2\times 3\times 3=18$portfolios. Low/Medium/High are the average returns on the six low/medium/high-STLEV$STLEV$andLTLEV$LTLEV$portfolios, respectively. Panel A covers all levered, nonfinancial NYSE, NASDAQ, and Amex firms, in total 964,984 monthly return observations for 10,202 firms. In Panel B, we exclude from the sorts almost-zero-leverage (AZL) firms, that is, firms with a leverage ratio of less than 5%, which results in 808,867 monthly return observations for 8,935 levered firms. Both samples cover the 1976 to 2019 period.

2.description: Cash flow beta and optimal financing choice. We compute for a cross-section of 500 firms the optimal financing choice. We fix all parameters except for the baseline liquidity spread,l0, and those that determine cash flow risk, namely, cash flow growth,μQ$\mu ^{\mathbb{Q}}$, cash flow beta,βX$\beta ^X$, and firm-specific volatility,σf$\sigma _f$. We uniformly draw these parameters in reasonable intervals. TableXprovides details on parameter choices. For each firm, we find the combination of total debt,P, and refinancing intensity, ϕ, that maximizes firm value,F(X0)$F(X_0)$. Panel A plots cash flow beta,βX$\beta ^X$, against leverage,L(X0)$L(X_0)$, and Panel B plots cash flow beta,βX$\beta ^X$, against refinancing intensity, ϕ.

3.description: Leverage, refinancing intensity, and equity returns. This figure shows the equity return implications of firms' optimal financing choices. We compute for a cross-section of 500 firms the optimal financing choice. We fix all parameters except for the baseline liquidity spread,l0, and those that determine cash flow risk, namely, cash flow growth,μQ$\mu ^{\mathbb{Q}}$, cash flow beta,βX$\beta ^X$, and firm-specific volatility,σf$\sigma _f$. We uniformly draw these parameters in reasonable intervals. TableXprovides details on parameter choices. For each firm, we find the combination of total debt,P, and refinancing intensity, ϕ, that maximizes firm value,F(X0)$F(X_0)$. In Panel A, we plot leverage,L(X0)$L(X_0)$, against the excess return on equity,R−r$R-r$, and in Panel B, we plot refinancing intensity, ϕ, against the excess return on equity,R−r$R-r$.

4.description: Long-term leverage, short-term leverage, and equity returns. This figure shows the equity return implications of firms' optimal financing choices. We compute for a cross-section of 500 firms the optimal financing choice. We fix all parameters except for the baseline liquidity spread,l0, and those that determine cash flow risk, namely, cash flow growth,μQ$\mu ^{\mathbb{Q}}$, cash flow beta,βX$\beta ^X$, and firm-specific volatility,σf$\sigma _f$. We uniformly draw these parameters in reasonable intervals. TableXprovides details on parameter choices. For each firm, we find the combination of total debt,P, and refinancing intensity, ϕ, that maximizes firm value,F(X0)$F(X_0)$. In Panel A, we plot long-term leverage,LTL(X0)=(1−ϕ)L(X0)$LTL(X_0) =(1-\phi ) L(X_0)$, against the excess return on equity,R−r$R-r$, whereL(X0)$L(X_0)$is the total leverage ratio and ϕ is the refinancing intensity, and in Panel B, we plot short-term leverage,STL(X0)=ϕL(X0)$STL(X_0) =\phi L(X_0)$, against the excess return on equity,R−r$R-r$.

5.description: Default probability, credit risk premium, and equity returns. This figure shows the equity return implications of firms' optimal financing choices. We compute for a cross-section of 500 firms the optimal financing choice. We fix all parameters except for the baseline liquidity spread,l0, and those that determine cash flow risk, namely, cash flow growth,μQ$\mu ^{\mathbb{Q}}$, cash flow beta,βX$\beta ^X$, and firm-specific volatility,σf$\sigma _f$. We uniformly draw these parameters in reasonable intervals. TableXprovides details on parameter choices. For each firm, we find the combination of total debt,P, and refinancing intensity, ϕ, that maximizes firm value,F(X0)$F(X_0)$. In Panel A, we plot the (log of the) physical default probability,log(πP)$\log (\pi ^{\mathbb{P}})$, against the excess return on equity,R−r$R-r$, and in Panel B, we plot the credit risk premium defined as the (log) difference in risk-neutral and physical default probabilities,log(πQ)−log(πP)$\log (\pi ^{\mathbb{Q}}) - \log (\pi ^{\mathbb{P}})$, against the excess return on equity,R−r$R-r$.
